elements.sent-2411-001: Distributional regression aims at estimating the conditional distribution of a target variable given explanatory co-variates.
elements.sent-2411-002: It is a crucial tool for forecasting when a precise uncertainty quantification is required.
elements.sent-2411-003: A popular methodology consists in fitting a parametric model via empirical risk minimization where the risk is measured by the Continuous Rank Probability Score (CRPS).
elements.sent-2411-004: For independent and identically distributed observations, we provide a concentration result for the estimation error and an upper bound for its expectation.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we consider model selection performed by minimization of the validation error and provide a concentration bound for the regret.
elements.sent-2411-006: A similar result is proved for convex aggregation of models.
elements.sent-2411-007: Finally, we show that our results may be applied to various models such as Ensemble Model Output Statistics (EMOS), distributional regression networks, distributional nearest neighbors or distributional random forests and we illustrate our findings on two data sets (QSAR aquatic toxicity and Airfoil self-noise).
elements.sent-2411-008: We consider in this paper the distributional regression problem, where we want to estimate the conditional distribution of a target random variable $Y$ given explanatory co-variates $X$.
elements.sent-2411-009: We assume $(X,Y)\in\mathbb{R ^d\times \mathbb{R}$ and we let \begin{equation}\label{eq:cdf} F^{*}_{x}(y)=\mathbb{P}\left(Y\leq y\,|\,X=x\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Estimation is built on a training sample $\mathcal{D _n=\{(X_i,Y_i),\,1\leq i\leq n\}$ of independent identically distributed (i.i.d.) copies of $(X,Y)$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Let us emphasize that distributional regression is much more challenging than standard regression where only a point prediction for $Y$ given $X=x$ is provided which typically reduces to an estimation of the conditional expectation.
elements.sent-2411-012: The conditional distribution provides a full account for the variability of $Y$ given $X=x$ and distributional regression is therefore a crucial tool for forecasting when a precise uncertainty quantification is required.
elements.sent-2411-013: Distributional regression is of relevance in various applied fields.
elements.sent-2411-014: To name a few, let us mention statistical post-processing of weather forecast~\citep{Matheson_Winkler76, Gneiting05 , forecasting of wind gusts~\citep{Baran2015 , of solar irradiance~\citep{Schulz2021 , of ICU length stays during COVID-19 pandemy~\citep{Henzi2021 , of breast cancer ODX score in oncology~\citep{AlMasry2024}\dots The methodology relies on probabilistic forecast where the forecaster produces a predictive distribution for the quantity of interest \citep{Gneiting_Katzfuss14 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Fitting a distributional regression model typically involves the minimization of a proper scoring rule, the most popular one being the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS, ~\citealp{Matheson_Winkler76, Gneiting_Raftery07 ).}
elements.sent-2411-016: It compares the actual observation with the predictive distribution in a comprehensive manner.
elements.sent-2411-017: Many different models have been proposed for distributional regression among which: Analog similar to a nearest neighbor method (KNN, \citealp{ZT89 ), Ensemble Model Output Statistics similar to Gaussian heteroscedastic regression (EMOS, \citealp{Gneiting_Raftery07 ), Isotonic Distributional Regression \citep{Henzi_et_al_2021 and more recent machine learning methods such as Distributional Regression Network (DRN, \citealp{Rasp_Lerch18 ) or Distributional Random Forest (DRF, \citealp{Cevid_MichelNBM22 ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Despite these successful achievements in terms of methods and applications, a sound theory for distributional regression via scoring rule minimization is still missing.
elements.sent-2411-019: Recently, minimax rates of convergence with CRPS-error have been considered for distributional regression \citep{Pic_Dombry_Naveau_Taillardat23 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The aim of this paper is to provide statistical learning guarantees for model fitting, model selection and convex aggregation based on CRPS minimization.
elements.sent-2411-021: Model selection and convex aggregation are very important techniques in the field of statistics and machine learning and have been used very successfully for regression function estimation, see~\citet{Tsybakov03,Bunea_Tsybakov_Wegkamp07 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The methods use two independent samples; the first sample, called training sample, is used to construct the initial estimators which are constituted as a dictionary (a collection of candidates).
elements.sent-2411-023: The second sample, called the validation sample, is used to aggregate them.
elements.sent-2411-024: Model selection enables the selection of the best candidate in the dictionary, while convex aggregation provides the optimal convex combination from these candidates.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our main results include a concentration bound for the theoretical risk when a parametric model is fitted via CRPS empirical risk minimization.
elements.sent-2411-026: We also consider model selection and model aggregation via CRPS minimization on a validation set and provide concentration bounds for the regret.
elements.sent-2411-027: Our results are first derived under sub-Gaussianity assumptions and then extended under weaker moment assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-028: We show that they apply to several popular models such as EMOS, DRN, KNN or DRF and provide a short illustration on two different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-029: Section~\ref{sec:background-drn-goals first provides some background on distributional regression and proper scoring rules and then presents precisely the main methods and goals.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The main results are stated in Section~\ref{sec:main-results : an oracle inequality for the estimation error in model fitting (Theorem~\ref{thm:estimation-error ), and concentration bounds for the regret in model selection (Theorem~\ref{thm:regret-model-selection ) and model aggregation (Theorem~\ref{thm:regret-model-aggregation ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In Section~\ref{sec:examples , some specific models are introduced and the assumptions for our results to hold are checked.}
elements.sent-2411-032: A short illustration on two different data set is provided in Section~\ref{sec:numerical-results .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Finally, an appendix contains all the proofs as well as some additional results.
elements.section-2411-002: Background on distributional regression and main goals
elements.subsection-2411-001: Probabilistic forecast and its evaluation with scoring rules
elements.sent-2411-034: We first consider the simple setting of probabilistic forecast without co-variate where a future observation $Y$ is predicted by a probability distribution $F$, called predictive distribution.
elements.sent-2411-035: Proper scoring rules are used in order to compare the predictive distribution $F$ and the materializing observation $y$ which are objects of different nature.
elements.sent-2411-036: Let $\mathcal{P _0\subset \mathcal{P (\mathbb{R )$ denote a subset of the set of all probability measures on $\mathbb{R}$, often identified with their c.d.f.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: A scoring rule\footnote{For the sake of simplicity, we consider only the case of real-valued observation $Y$ and of non-negative scoring rule.}
elements.sent-2411-038: A more general definition can be found in \cite{Gneiting_Raftery07 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: The quantity $S(F,y)$ is interpreted as the error between the predictive distribution $F$ and the materializing observation $y$.
elements.sent-2411-040: The mean error when $Y$ has "true" distribution $G$ is denoted by \begin{equation}\bar S(F,G)=E_{Y\sim G}[S(F,Y)].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-041: The following notion of proper and strictly proper scoring rule is central in the theory.
elements.sent-2411-042: The scoring rule $S$ is said proper on $\mathcal{P _0$ when \begin{equation}\label{eq:def-proper} \bar S(F,G)\geq \bar S(G,G), \quad \mbox{for all $F,G\in\mathcal{P}_0$}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-043: It is said strictly proper when equality in Eq.~\eqref{eq:def-proper implies $F=G$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Stated differently, the scoring rule $S$ is strictly proper on $\mathcal{P _0$ when \begin{equation}\mathop{arg\,min}_{F\inP_0}\bar S(F,G)=\{G\},\quad \mbox{for all $G\inP_0$}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The interpretation is that, in order to minimize its mean error, the forecaster has to predict the "true" observation distribution $G$.
elements.sent-2411-046: In this paper, we consider the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS, \citealt{Matheson_Winkler76 ).}
elements.sent-2411-047: This scoring rule is defined by the formula \begin{equation}\label{eq:CRPS} S(F,y)= \int_{\mathbb{R}}\left(\mathds{1}_{\{y\leq z\}}-F(z)\right)^2\mathrm{d}z\end{equation} for all $F$ finite absolute moment.
elements.sent-2411-048: Here the subset $\mathcal{P _0\subset\mathcal{P (\mathbb{R )$ is the Wasserstein space \begin{equation}\label{eq:def-P1} \mathcal{P}_1(\mathbb{R})=\Big\{F\in\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})\colon m_1(F)=\int_{\mathbb{R}}|y|F(\mathrm{d}y)<\infty\Big\}\end{equation} of probability measures on $\mathbb{R}$ with finite first moment.}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: One can easily check from this definition that \begin{equation}\bar S(F,G)=\int_{R}G(z)\left(1-G(z)\right)dz+\int_{R}\left(F(z)-G(z)\right)^2dz,\end{equation} which implies \begin{equation}\label{eq:divergence-crps} \bar S(F,G)-\bar S(G,G)=\int_{\mathbb{R}}\left(F(z)-G(z)\right)^2\mathrm{d}z.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-050: This quantity is nonnegative and vanishes if and only if $F=G$, ensuring that the $\mathrm{CRPS}$ is a strictly proper scoring rule.
elements.sent-2411-051: For discrete predictive distributions of the form $F=\sum_{i=1 ^n w_i\delta_{y_i}$ (with $\delta_y$ the Dirac mass at $y$), the $\mathrm{CRPS}$ can be computed simply \citep{Gneiting_Raftery07 by \begin{equation}S(F,y)= \sum_{i=1}^n w_i|y_i-y| -\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i\neq j}w_iw_j|y_i-y_j|.\end{equation}.}}

elements.section-2411-004: Examples and popular models for distributional regression

elements.subsection-2411-007: Distributional k-Nearest Neighbors and Random Forests
elements.sent-2411-140: The predictive distribution is built on a straightforward extension of $k$-nearest neighbor regression: we set \begin{equation}\hat F_{n,x}(y)=\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^k \mathds{1}_{\{X_i\in knn(x)\}} \mathds{1}_{\{Y_i\leq y\}}\end{equation} where $\mathrm{knn (x)$ denotes the set of $k$ nearest neighbors of $x$ in the training set $(X_i)_{1\leq i\leq n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-141: The main hyperparameter is the number $k$ of neighbors, leading to a model selection problem as considered in~\ref{sec:model-selection-aggregation .}
elements.sent-2411-142: This simple method is known as the Analog method in the framework of statistical post-processing of weather forecast \citep{ZT89 .}
elements.sent-2411-143: Random forests for distributional regression have been introduced by \cite{Cevid_MichelNBM22 and are a powerful nonparametric method.}
elements.sent-2411-144: We describe only the main lines of the method.
elements.sent-2411-145: Recall that in standard regression, Breiman's Random Forest \citep{Breiman_2001 estimates the regression function by \begin{equation}\hat \mu(x)=\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^B T^{(b)}(x)=\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^B \frac{1}{|L^b(x)|}\sum_{i=1}^n Y_i\mathds{1}_{\{X_i\in L^b(x)\}},\end{equation} where $T_1,\ldots,T_B$ denote randomized regression trees built on bootstrap samples of the original data and $L^b(x)$ the leaf containing $x$ in the tree $T^b$.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Interverting the two sums yields \begin{equation}\hat \mu(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^B \frac{\mathds{1}_{\{X_i\in L^b(x)\}}}{|L^b(x)|}\right)Y_i=\sum_{i=1}^n w_{ni}(x)Y_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-147: Using these Random Forest weights, the predictive distribution writes \begin{equation}\hat F_{n,x}(y)=\sum_{i=1}^n w_{ni}(x)\mathds{1}_{\{Y_i\leq y\}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-148: This strategy based on Breiman's regression tree is used by \citet{meinshausen_2006 to construct the quantile regression forest.}
elements.sent-2411-149: Different splitting strategies in the tree construction have been considered to better detect changes in distribution rather than changes in mean (\citealt{Taillarda_Mestre_Zamo_Naveau16, Atheyetal2019 ).}
elements.sent-2411-150: Note that \cite{Cevid_MichelNBM22 minimizes a scoring rule to construct the splits of the trees.}
elements.sent-2411-151: The main hyperparameter of this nonparametric method is the so-called mtry parameter that controls the number of co-variates tested at each split in the trees.

elements.sent-2411-155: In this section, we illustrate how model selection and model aggregation work on real data sets.
elements.sent-2411-156: The cross validation methodology, widely used in practice, is justified by the theoretical results obtained in Section~\ref{sec:main-results .}
elements.sent-2411-157: The source code for these experiments can be found at \url{https://github.com/ZaouiAmed/Neurips2024_DistributionalRegression .}
elements.sent-2411-158: We consider two datasets used in the framework of heteroscedastic regression with reject option as detailed in~\citet{Zaoui2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: The first dataset, QSAR aquatic toxicity \citep{qsar is referred to as qsar and comprises $546$ observations with $8$ numerical features used to predict acute toxicity in Pimephales promelas.}
elements.sent-2411-160: The toxicity output ranges from $0.12$ to $10.05$ with evidence of a low heteroscedasticity.
elements.sent-2411-161: The second dataset, Airfoil Self-Noise \citep{airfoil is referred to as airfoil and consists of $1503$ observations with $5$ measured features from aerodynamic and acoustic tests.}
elements.sent-2411-162: The output represents the scaled sound pressure level in decibels, ranging from $103$ to $140$ with evidence of a strong heteroscedasticity..
elements.sent-2411-163: We consider the KNN and DRF models to predict the conditional distribution of the output variable.
elements.sent-2411-164: Our focus lies on hyperparameters selection via minimization of the validation error, where the main hyperparameter is the number k of neighbors and the number mtry of variables considered at each split for KNN and DRF respectively.
elements.sent-2411-165: We utilize the implementation of these methods from the R packages KernelKnn \cite{ and DRF \cite{ .}}
elements.sent-2411-166: For DRF, a preliminary exploration shows that sensible choices for the other parameters are num.trees$=1000$, sample.fraction=$0.9$, min.node.size$=1$ and default values for others parameters.
elements.sent-2411-167: Finally, the R package ScoringRule is used for CRPS computation and the optim function based on the Nelder-Mead method is used for parameter optimization in convex aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-168: We use the same methodology for the two datasets.
elements.sent-2411-169: We divide the data into three parts: $50\%$ for training, $20\%$ for validation and $30\%$ for testing.
elements.sent-2411-170: In a first stage, the training set is used to train the model (KNN or DRF) for the various hyperparameters (k or mtry) and the validation set is used to select best hyperparameters $\widehat{k}$ or $\widehat{mtry}$.
elements.sent-2411-171: Furthermore, the validation set also used to choose for model selection (MS), choosing between KNN and RF, and the best convex aggregation (CA).
elements.sent-2411-172: In a second stage, the different models (KNN, RF, MS and CA) are refitted on the union of training and validation sets ($70\%$ of data) and evaluated on the test set ($30\%$) by computing the test CRPS-error.
elements.sent-2411-173: This process is repeated $100$ times for different random splits of the data into training, validation and testing sets.
elements.sent-2411-174: The distributions of the test error for KNN, DRF, MS and CA is then analyzed in terms of mean, standard error and boxplot.
elements.sent-2411-175: For the sake of brevity, only the results for the qsar dataset are presented, while the results for the airfoil dataset are postponed to Appendix~\ref{app:additionnal-numerical-results .}
elements.sent-2411-176: The first stage of the procedure where the hyperparameter (k or mtry) is selected by minimization of the validation error is shown on the left plot of Fig.~\ref{fig:qsar-val for KNN and on the middle plot for DRF.}
elements.sent-2411-177: In both cases, the validation error curve shows a clear minimum allowing to select the hyperparameter $\widehat{k =8$ and $\widehat{mtry =4$ corresponding to CRPS-error of $0.696$ and $0.678$ on the validation set respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-178: In the second stage, KNN, DRF, MS and CA are evaluated on the test set and the right plot of Fig.~\ref{fig:qsar-val shows the distribution of the test error over 100 repetitions.}
elements.sent-2411-179: We can see from the boxplots that the distribution of the test error is slightly larger for KNN than for DRF, that MS achieves almost the same performance as RF and that CA achieves slightly better performance than DRF.
elements.sent-2411-180: Hence model selection and convex aggregation accomplish their goal.

elements.sent-2411-182: In this work, we considered distributional regression with error assessed using the CRPS and investigated model fitting via empirical risk minimization.
elements.sent-2411-183: Additionally, we explored classical aggregation procedures: model selection and convex aggregation where two independent samples are used, the first for model construction, the second for model selection or convex aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-184: We derived oracle concentration inequalities for the estimation error and established an upper bound for its expectation within the sub-Gaussian framework and beyond, considering weaker moment assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-185: These new theoretical results are solid mathematical justifications for common practices in the framework of distributional regression.
